moveAnnotations.annotation: White seizes the center with the king's pawn, claiming d5 and f5 and opening lines for the bishop and queen. The most popular opening move — it establishes a foothold that Black must address immediately.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single e4 pawn. White plans d4 next for a commanding two-pawn center.
plans: Build a d4-e4 pawn center
Develop pieces rapidly behind the strong center
alternatives: 1.d4 — leads to queen's pawn openings, avoids the Pirc entirely

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Pirc Defense starts here. Black plays ...d6 instead of challenging the center immediately, saying 'go ahead, build your big center — I'll undermine it later.' This is a hypermodern approach: Black lets White overextend and then strikes from the flanks. The d6 pawn controls e5 and prepares a kingside fianchetto.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn is modest but flexible. It controls e5 and supports a later ...e5 or ...c5 break depending on how White sets up.
plans: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 to pressure White's center from the flank
Prepare ...e5 or ...c5 to challenge the center when the time is right
alternatives: 1...e5 — the most direct reply, immediately fighting for the center
1...c5 (Sicilian) — creates asymmetry and fights for d4

moveAnnotations.annotation: White doesn't hesitate — two pawns in the center, maximum space. The d4-e4 duo controls everything: c5, d5, e5, f5. Against the Pirc, this is practically automatic. White will try to use this space advantage to develop quickly and launch an attack before Black can chip away at the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The classical d4-e4 center gives White significant space advantage. The risk is that this big center can become a target if Black challenges it effectively with ...c5 or ...e5.
plans: Develop pieces behind the center and prepare a kingside attack
Push f4 to build an even bigger center and threaten f5

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to f6, pressuring e4 and continuing the hypermodern strategy. The knight on f6 is flexible — it eyes e4 and d5, and doesn't commit to a particular pawn structure yet. Black is saying: 'I'll develop pieces first, challenge your center second.'
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change. Black keeps maximum flexibility for the structure.
plans: Fianchetto with ...g6 and ...Bg7 next
Castle kingside quickly and then attack the center
alternatives: 2...g6 — transposes to the same thing usually, but Nf6 first keeps options open

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to protect e4 and control d5. This is the natural developing move — the knight on c3 is the backbone of White's center, defending e4 and preparing to support f4 if White goes for the Austrian Attack. Every piece comes out with a purpose.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The center is fully supported. Nc3 makes e4 rock-solid and prepares expansive setups.
plans: Play f4 for the Austrian Attack — the most aggressive option
Develop Nf3 and Be2 for a quieter classical setup
alternatives: 3.f3 — preparing a big center with Be3 and Qd2, the Saemisch-like approach

moveAnnotations.annotation: The fianchetto begins. Black will place the bishop on g7 where it becomes a monster — aiming straight at White's d4 pawn along the long diagonal. This is the heart of the Pirc: the g7 bishop, combined with ...e5 or ...c5, will create enormous pressure on White's center. The king will also be safe after ...O-O.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The g6 pawn creates a shelter for the king and prepares the fianchetto. Black's kingside is being built as a fortress.
plans: Complete the fianchetto with ...Bg7
Castle quickly and then look for ...e5 or ...c5 counterplay

moveAnnotations.annotation: The Austrian Attack! White plays f4, building the most aggressive pawn center possible: d4-e4-f4. This is a direct attempt to crush Black before the fianchetto becomes effective. White's pawns control a huge amount of territory and f5 is a natural follow-up to crack open Black's kingside. This is the sharpest test of the Pirc.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The d4-e4-f4 pawn trio is powerful but commits White's f-pawn early. If this center gets undermined, the f4 pawn can become a weakness, especially with the king on the same side.
plans: Develop quickly and push e5 to kick the f6 knight and gain more space
Use the f4-f5 advance to attack Black's kingside
alternatives: 4.Nf3 — the Classical system, less aggressive but very solid

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop takes its throne on g7. This is the most important piece in the Pirc — it pressures d4 along the long diagonal, defends the kingside, and becomes incredibly powerful if Black can ever open the center with ...e5 or ...c5. Think of it as a sniper aimed at White's center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No pawn change, but the bishop on g7 adds dynamic pressure to White's d4 pawn, which is the base of the center.
plans: Castle immediately and get the king safe
Prepare ...c5 or ...e5 to blow open the long diagonal for the bishop

moveAnnotations.annotation: White completes minor piece development. The knight on f3 supports d4, controls e5, and prepares castling. White has a massive center and good development — now it's about converting this into an attack. The Nf3 placement is solid, though some players prefer Bd3 first.
plans: Castle kingside and prepare e5 or f5 advances
Play Bd3 to develop the bishop actively
alternatives: 5.e5 — immediate central advance, very sharp but premature

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles immediately — getting the king to safety is urgent when White has the Austrian Attack setup. With f4 already played, White has attacking potential against the kingside, so Black needs the king tucked away and the rook on f8 where it supports a potential ...f5 counter-strike.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The king is safe behind the g6-f7-h7 pawns. Black's structure is compact and ready for counterplay.
plans: Play ...c5 to attack White's d4 pawn
Prepare ...e5 to challenge the center head-on

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to d3, the most natural square in the Austrian Attack. From d3, the bishop eyes the kingside (particularly h7), supports e4, and can facilitate a later e5 push. It's also well-placed if White ever plays f5 to open lines against Black's king.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No structural change. The bishop supports the central pawns and prepares for kingside operations.
plans: Castle and then launch a kingside attack with e5 or f5
Use the d3-h7 diagonal for attacking chances
alternatives: 6.Be2 — safer but less aggressive, the bishop is passive on e2

moveAnnotations.annotation: An unusual-looking move, but it's actually very smart. The knight goes to a6 to reroute to c7 and then either e6 or help with ...c5. Why not Nc6? Because on c6 the knight blocks the c-pawn, and Black absolutely needs ...c5 to challenge White's big center. Na6-c7 keeps the c-file clear for the critical counter-break.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black preserves the ability to play ...c5, which is the most important structural break in the Pirc.
plans: Reroute the knight via c7 to support ...c5 or reach e6
Play ...c5 to challenge the d4 pawn and activate the g7 bishop
alternatives: 6...Nc6 — more natural looking but blocks the c-pawn, making ...c5 impossible

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles, getting the king safe and connecting the rooks. With both sides castled on the same wing, it's a race: White wants to attack on the kingside with e5 or f5, while Black wants to undermine the center with ...c5. White's space advantage means the attack tends to come first.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change. White's pawn center remains dominant and the king is safe.
plans: Play e5 to gain more space and kick the f6 knight
Push f5 to pry open Black's kingside

moveAnnotations.annotation: The critical counter-strike! Black hits d4 with ...c5, the whole reason the knight went to a6 instead of c6. This is the main source of counterplay in the Pirc — by attacking d4, Black forces White to make a decision about the center. If White takes, the g7 bishop becomes a monster on the open diagonal. If White pushes d5, the position closes.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The c5 pawn challenges d4 directly. If dxc5 dxc5, Black gets an open d-file and a strong bishop on g7. If d5, the position becomes more closed and strategic.
plans: Open the long diagonal for the g7 bishop
Create counterplay on the queenside and in the center
alternatives: 7...e5 — also possible, but ...c5 is the more standard and flexible break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White closes the center with d5. This is a committal decision — White locks the structure, which reduces the power of Black's g7 bishop (the long diagonal is blocked) but gains space and a protected passed pawn potential. The game now becomes about White attacking on the kingside while Black seeks counterplay on the queenside with ...a6, ...b5, ...Rb8.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Closed center with pawns on d5-e4-f4 vs d6-c5. White has a space advantage but the center is locked, reducing tactical sharpness.
plans: Attack on the kingside with f5 or e5
Use the space advantage to build pressure
alternatives: 8.dxc5 dxc5 — opens the position, which actually favors Black's bishop on g7

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight completes its journey to c7, exactly as planned from a6. From c7, the knight supports ...b5 and can jump to e8-f6 or a6-b4 as needed. It also keeps an eye on the d5 pawn and supports ...e6 to challenge the center. This is a typical Pirc maneuver — the knight looks awkward but it's very functional.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black is preparing ...b5 and queenside counterplay. The knight on c7 is perfectly placed to support this plan.
plans: Play ...a6 and ...b5 for queenside expansion
Prepare ...e6 to challenge the d5 pawn when ready
alternatives: 8...Nc7 is essentially the only move — the knight needs to get off a6
